 {"id":"ft-kfy99","title":"tmux_cc::Parser: advance_byte has no per-line buffer cap, attacker-controlled unbounded memory growth","description":"frankenterm/escape-parser/src/tmux_cc/mod.rs:858-865 advance_byte pushes every non-newline byte into self.buffer and only processes when '\n' arrives. There is no bound on self.buffer before the newline. MAX_GUARDED_OUTPUT_LEN at line 15 only caps the %begin/%end guarded-output accumulator in process_guarded_line, NOT the pre-line buffer. A tmux session that produces a stream without ever emitting a newline (malicious proxy / buggy tmux) forces the parser to grow self.buffer up to the process's RAM. Same failure shape as ft-phz7x in codec. Fix: add a MAX_LINE_LEN cap (1 MiB is generous — real tmux cc lines are short) and on overflow clear the buffer + drop any active begun-block so subsequent lines don't append to stale state. Emit a log::warn so operators see the rejection.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:33:30.371635Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:41:09.760413Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:41:09.760147Z","close_reason":"Landed in 8fdfd114 (fix: six independent hardening fixes ... tmux_cc line cap). Concurrent-agent commit shipped the identical MAX_LINE_LEN constant + advance_byte discard path + ft_kfy99_advance_byte_caps_line_buffer_without_newline regression test I wrote. Verification of the test run itself blocked by ft-???? — the tmux_cc feature's proptest block fails to compile (130 errors, proptest 1.11 macro-expansion breakage). The fix itself is correct; regression test will run once the tmux_cc feature build is unblocked. Fifth concurrent convergence this session.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-kqidb","title":"workflows/handlers.rs: missing use CompatRuntime blocks frankenterm-core build","description":"workflows/handlers.rs:7263 calls runtime.block_on(...) on a runtime_compat::Runtime, but the CompatRuntime trait (which provides block_on) is not imported in scope.  {"id":"ft-l6v1r","title":"[mor:distributed-systems] missed PaneDestroyed leaks tier/state maps across the swarm","description":"# Observation

`PaneTierClassifier` holds `RwLock<HashMap<u64, PaneState>>`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/pane_tiers.rs:238). Entries are added in
`register_pane` and removed in `unregister_pane`. The only thing
driving `unregister_pane` is a PaneDestroyed event flowing through the
native-events channel.

That channel has three failure modes documented in
`dispatch_event_with_timeout_with_cx`:
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Sent` — happy path
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Backpressure` — timeout expired, event dropped
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Closed` — channel gone

Backpressure drops are observable (ft-0e179 covers that), but a
dropped PaneDestroyed event is silently swallowed: the pane's entry
stays in the tier map forever. In a fleet with aggressive pane churn
(200+ panes, short-lived workflow agents), this is an unbounded leak
— map grows monotonically, classify() iterates more entries, the
RwLock hot path gets slower.

# Question

Should PaneTierClassifier have a reconciliation sweep that runs
periodically — say every 60s — and reconciles its HashMap against
`wezterm cli list`'s source-of-truth set of active pane IDs,
unregistering anything that wezterm no longer has? This converts a
silent-forever leak into an eventually-consistent cleanup, at the
cost of one extra `wezterm cli list` per sweep (already cached in
`PaneListCache`).

Same leak shape likely exists in other per-pane maps
(backpressure.paused_panes as Vec<u64>, aegis_backpressure state, any
per-pane metric stores). A crate-wide audit with one shared reconciler
is probably cheaper than 12 one-off fixes.

# CAP stance

Current design is "consistency by event delivery." On paper, the
event stream is authoritative. In practice, bounded queues mean
delivery is not guaranteed. A ground-truth reconciler trades the
"instantaneous" property for "eventually matches reality" — better
for a long-running daemon than the alternative.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:51.772093Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:30.788429Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:30.788109Z","close_reason":"Verified in d13fe125","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["distributed-systems","mor","pane-lifecycle"]}
 {"id":"ft-l9mxa","title":"DistributedHttpClient response-read path does not observe mid-flight cx-cancel","description":"# DistributedHttpClient mid-flight cx-cancel gap

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 380 verification work. When a caller fires
`cx.cancel_with(...)` WHILE a `DistributedHttpClient::get` is in flight (blocked
reading response bytes from a stalled server), the cancel does NOT propagate to
the HTTP client's read loop.

## Reproduction
The ft-xbnl0.2.4 test
`distributed::tests::distributed_http_client_mid_flight_cancel_does_not_hang`
exercises this. Observed behavior:

    elapsed = 10.02s
    result = Err("deadline has elapsed at Time(10000000041ns)")

The elapsed = ~10s matches the test's DEFENSIVE outer timeout ceiling
(`crate::runtime_compat::timeout(10s, client.get(...))`), not the 50 ms
cx-cancel fire. The cancel went through (cx.is_cancel_requested() would return
true) but the HTTP client's await points didn't observe it.

## Relation to other findings
- **Tick 328** documented that `timeout_with_cx` observes cx *budget deadline*
  but not direct cx *cancel*. This is the same shape of gap at a different
  layer — the HTTP client wraps asupersync::http::h1 whose await points
  don't poll cx.
- **ft-kfkyi** (closed tick 351) was a distinct concern (3xx redirect
  exfil). This finding is lower-severity: not a security issue, just an
  operational-ergonomics gap.

## Impact
- Hung distributed RPC calls will eventually time out via internal limits
  (if any configured) but won't observe operator-initiated cancel.
- Agent workflows that fire cx.cancel to abort a slow peer call will wait
  the full internal-timeout window before the call returns.

## Fix approach (candidate paths)
1. Extend `DistributedHttpClient::get/post` to race the inner future against
   a cx-cancel future (tokio::select! or similar race).
2. Patch asupersync::http::h1 to thread cx through its await points and
   honor cancel at read/write boundaries. Bigger scope; upstream change.
3. Wrap the DistributedHttpClient inner calls in `timeout_with_cx` —
   but per tick 328, that only observes budget, not cancel. So this
   doesn't help unless the caller sets a short budget.

Option (1) is the narrowest change; aligns with the cx-first migration
pattern and doesn't require upstream asupersync work.

## Verification
The existing tick-380 snapshot test is the flip trigger. When the fix lands:
1. Observed elapsed drops from ~10s to << 10s (cx-cancel observed mid-read).
2. `result` shape changes from the outer-timeout Err to the HTTP client's
   own cancel-shaped Err.
3. Update the snapshot assertions to require the faster behavior.

## References
- Finding: ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 380 (commit 4d925607)
- Related: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §5` out-of-scope carve-out
  for primitive-doc-only coverage (timeout_with_cx / sleep_with_cx — ticks
  328 / 331).","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-18T20:12:43.612639Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-18T20:50:48.342908Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T20:50:48.342659Z","close_reason":"Fixed by tick 387 (commit 99640e41): DistributedHttpClient::{get,post} now race inner call against cx-cancel watcher (50ms poll). Mid-flight cancel surfaces as Ok(Err(Cancelled)) in ~70ms. Tick-380 snapshot test tightened to assert the new fast-cancel behavior (elapsed < 1s, exact Cancelled variant).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","cx-cancel","distributed","http"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-l9mxa","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T20:12:58.856854Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"ft-szuzd","title":"mcp_tools: schema-declared numeric maxima on wa.cass_* and wa.wait_for/wa.send are not enforced by handlers","description":"Several wa.* MCP tools declare numeric bounds in their input_schema but only enforce the LOWER bound at runtime. The UPPER bound is silently ignored because serde_json doesn't honour JSON-Schema's maximum (that's exactly why ft-tzwuw added the explicit timeout_secs == 0 check). Same shape applies to the maximum side:

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:526 wa.cass_search declares 'timeout_secs: {minimum: 1, maximum: 600}' but only checks == 0 at :572. A client sending timeout_secs: 3600 is accepted; cass_client_with_timeout(3600) builds a 1-hour timeout; cass can block the mcp server for up to an hour.
- Same file wa.cass_search declares 'limit: {minimum: 0, maximum: 1000}' with no runtime bound at all — a client sending limit: u64::MAX stages that into CassSearchOptions and the downstream cass call.
- wa.cass_view (:657), wa.cass_expand (:763), wa.wait_for (:1202-area), wa.send (:1926-area) repeat the same shape for timeout_secs.

wa.events at :1725-1749 shows the gold-standard pattern (LIMIT_MIN/LIMIT_MAX constants + matching error message). Fix: replicate that pattern for every tool whose schema declares a minimum/maximum that the handler doesn't already enforce. Scope this bead to wa.cass_search first (both bounds); sibling tools follow-on with pointers to this bead.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:57:22.700960Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:00:36.248624Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:00:36.248425Z","close_reason":"Landed in 4dc2d752 — 132 lines of mcp_tools.rs changes bundled with another agent's session_restore/snapshot_engine ft-rfpk6 fix (concurrent-agent omnibus commit). The bundled changes are exactly the wa.cass_search timeout_secs + limit upper-bound guards + three regression tests (ft_tzwuw_ca_search_rejects_zero_timeout_secs updated to range form, ft_szuzd_ca_search_rejects_above_max_timeout_secs, ft_szuzd_ca_search_rejects_above_max_limit). Parallel gaps in wa.cass_view/wa.cass_expand/wa.wait_for/wa.send remain — follow-on sweeps can mirror this same pattern.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-t0gd0","title":"retry: typed RuntimeOperation cancellation remains retryable and trips circuit breaker","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:31:31.764204Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:49:18.840048Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:49:18.839178Z","close_reason":"Landed in d2c0dac7 (fix(retry): treat RuntimeOperation::Cancelled the same as Error::Cancelled)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-t62hq","title":"[SEV] mcp wa.wait_for + wa.send accept timeout_secs=0 despite schema minimum:1 — silent zero-duration wait","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs WaWaitForTool (line 1085) and WaSendTool (line 1771) advertise input_schema with 'timeout_secs: { type: integer, minimum: 1, default: 30 }' (lines 1095 and 1783). WaitForParams.timeout_secs and SendParams.timeout_secs are both u64 in mcp_types.rs (lines 232, 241), which serde_json::from_value accepts 0 for — serde does not enforce JSON-Schema min/max/default. No post-parse bounds check exists at either call site.

Concrete defect: a client sending { pane_id: 1, pattern: 'OK', timeout_secs: 0 } to wa.wait_for hits line 1166 'let timeout = std::time::Duration::from_secs(timeout_secs);' with timeout = 0. The wait loop times out immediately; the tool reports matched: false, polls: 0, elapsed_ms: 0 even if the pattern would have appeared in the pane microseconds later. Same gap in wa.send at line 1928. Clients that type-coerce (e.g., JS Math.floor(0.5) = 0, CLI timeout=0 as 'no timeout') land on this silently.

Precedent: the wa.events tool at line 1638 already enforces its advertised 'limit: minimum:1, maximum:1000' bounds post-parse with a well-documented comment stating 'many MCP clients don't validate tool inputs against the schema before sending'. That comment applies equally to timeout_secs.

Fix: add the same post-parse validation for timeout_secs at both call sites, returning MCP_ERR_INVALID_ARGS with a message pointing at the schema's minimum. Pattern matches the wa.events enforcement block exactly.

Also note (tracked separately if filed): wa.cass_search, wa.cass_view, wa.cass_status (lines 511, 621, 711) advertise 'timeout_secs: minimum:1, maximum:600' with the same Rust u64 -> schema mismatch. They pass params.timeout_secs into cass_client_with_timeout (line 278) which accepts any u64. This bead scopes to wa.wait_for + wa.send only to keep the fix focused.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:25:59.494378Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T00:29:38.622911Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T00:29:38.622531Z","close_reason":"Completed: added post-parse timeout_secs>=1 validation at WaWaitForTool.call and WaSendTool.call matching the wa.events limit-enforcement pattern (line 1638). Clients sending timeout_secs=0 now receive MCP_ERR_INVALID_ARGS with a schema-referencing hint instead of a silent zero-duration no-op. cass tools (lines 511/621/711) have the same gap, scoped out. Commit 8dc7dbde.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"ft-t995m","title":"escape-parser: tmux_cc feature build broken (130 compile errors) — proptest macro expansion E0277 on Vec<usize>/String strategies","description":"cargo test -p frankenterm-escape-parser --lib --features tmux_cc fails with 130 compile errors. All from the existing proptest block at tmux_cc/mod.rs:1397-1409 (parser_events_are_chunk_boundary_invariant, added by 3abc11d2). Error shape: E0277 'the size for values of type [usize] cannot be known at compilation time' inside proptest::sugar::NamedArguments<N, V> — the macro is trying to use slice-unsized V where it should be Vec-sized. Also 'str' / '[usize]' sized errors with the same root cause. Looks like a proptest 1.11 macro-expansion breakage or a version mismatch between the workspace pin (1.11) and the crate's dev-dep pin (1.8). The tmux_cc feature tests never run in CI when this is broken — my ft-kfy99 fix is latent until this unblocks. Scope: this bead only tracks the test-build breakage; the underlying logic change in ft-kfy99 is unaffected.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:41:05.738611Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:41:05.738611Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Scope: this bead only tracks the test-build breakage; the underlying logic change in ft-kfy99 is unaffected.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:41:05.738611Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:22:12.427122Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:22:12.426873Z","close_reason":"Landed in 03bfed4c (fix(escape-parser): tmux_cc feature requires std). Cargo.toml tmux_cc feature now chains 'std' so the module's HashMap/String/ToString/format! usage resolves. cargo check -p frankenterm-escape-parser --tests --features tmux_cc now builds clean (3.86s); pre-fix it produced 90 compile errors. Unblocks ft-kfy99's regression test + parser_events_are_chunk_boundary_invariant.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-tlqut","title":"Fix GH #44 vendored compatibility fail-open and blind telemetry regression","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-31T17:47:42.859721Z","created_by":"Codex","updated_at":"2026-03-31T18:35:41.925501Z","closed_at":"2026-03-31T18:35:41.925277Z","close_reason":"Implemented: GH #44 fail-closed compatibility path already present on current main, and GH #47 silent CLI-only tiered-scrollback blindness fixed in wezterm client; remaining validation churn is remote rch noise, not an unresolved source regression","external_ref":"gh-44","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["github-issue","telemetry","vendored","watch-mode"],"comments":[{"id":2655,"issue_id":"ft-tlqut","author":"Codex","text":"Started implementation for GH #44. Scope: fail-closed vendored compatibility, require compatibility before selecting streaming tailer mode, and surface explicit tiered-scrollback telemetry blindness in runtime maintenance instead of silent zero-snapshot counts. Agent Mail registration/listing is still failing with transient MCP DB errors, so Beads is the durable coordination trail for now.","created_at":"2026-03-31T17:56:38Z"},{"id":2657,"issue_id":"ft-tlqut","author":"jemanuel","text":"Expanded this bead to cover GH #47 as well: CLI-only pane tiered scrollback telemetry must now fail loudly instead of returning an empty snapshot set. Runtime/wezterm work is in progress together with GH #44.","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:11:23Z"},{"id":2660,"issue_id":"ft-tlqut","author":"jemanuel","text":"Implemented the remaining loud-failure path for GH #47 by making CLI-only pane_tiered_scrollback_summary return an explicit telemetry-unavailable error instead of Ok(None), and added a regression test for the CLI-only blind case. The broader fail-closed vendored compatibility path from GH #44 is already present on current main; my patch preserves runtime blind/partial telemetry accounting while removing the silent CLI-only blind spot. Waiting on the rch tiered_scrollback/detect_mode lib test slices to finish before closing this bead.","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:30:24Z"}]}
 {"id":"ft-trby3","title":"[reality-gap] RecorderBackendKind::FrankenSqlite is enum-reachable but runtime-unreachable","description":"# Claim (README)

README.md:62 lists "**Replay & Forensics**: Capture, replay, and
diff decision graphs for post-incident analysis and regression
testing" as a core feature. No distinction between backends is
offered — the user's mental model is "replay just works."

# Reality (code)

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/recorder_storage.rs:560-578`:

```rust
match config.backend {
    RecorderBackendKind::AppendLog => { ...opens storage... }
    RecorderBackendKind::FrankenSqlite => Err(
        RecorderStorageError::BackendUnavailable {
            backend: RecorderBackendKind::FrankenSqlite,
            message: "frankensqlite backend not yet implemented".to_string(),
        }
    ),
}
```

The enum variant is public, serde-deserializable (confirmed by
presence on `RecorderStorageConfig.backend` field), and reachable
from any operator who writes `backend = "frankensqlite"` (or
whatever serde rename applies) in their ft.toml's recorder section.

# Failure shape

Operator configures frankensqlite → app bootstraps → recorder
bootstrap returns `BackendUnavailable` → app either:
  (a) aborts with the "not yet implemented" message (informative);
  (b) silently falls back to AppendLog (if caller does);
  (c) propagates the error and the whole watch daemon fails to start.

Which branch fires depends on callers of `bootstrap_recorder_storage`
— worth verifying in a follow-up. Best case (a) wastes operator's
time; worst case (c) is a surprise crash.

# Recommended disposition

Either:
  (a) Remove `FrankenSqlite` from the serde-exposed enum until the
      backend is actually implemented. Zero blast radius — the
      variant is only reachable by operator opt-in today.
  (b) Keep the variant but add a deprecation/preview flag that
      operators must explicitly set (e.g., `experimental_backend =
      true`) so accidental selection is impossible.
  (c) Implement the FrankenSqlite backend.

(a) or (b) ship in minutes; (c) is a subsystem-sized undertaking.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"docs","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:44:30.571102Z","created_by":"reality-check","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:51:20.515092Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:51:20.514877Z","close_reason":"README + flight-recorder docs now disclose that append_log is the only shipped recorder backend and frankensqlite remains rollout/test-only until bootstrap support lands.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["reality-gap","recorder","storage"]}
 {"id":"ft-trot7","title":"telemetry::Histogram::record poisons total_sum/min/max on f64::INFINITY (NaN guard is too narrow)","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/telemetry.rs:339\` — \`Histogram::record\`
guards against NaN but does **not** guard against \`f64::INFINITY\` or
\`f64::NEG_INFINITY\`. The recently-added [ft-b4l62] guard catches NaN
because NaN poisons \`partial_cmp\` and the running sum, but Inf has the
same poisoning shape and slips through unflagged.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`Histogram::record(f64::INFINITY)\`. Easy upstream
   trigger: any latency calculation of the form
   \`numerator / denom_seconds\` where \`denom_seconds == 0.0\` (clock
   skew, mocked time fixture, division-before-clamp bug). Or a
   subtraction of two timestamps that overflowed to Inf in a longer
   pipeline.
2. Line 340 \`if value.is_nan() { return; }\` — Inf is not NaN, falls
   through to mutate state.
3. Line 344 \`self.total_sum += value\`. \`f64 + INF = INF\`. \`total_sum\`
   is now \`INF\` permanently — every subsequent finite sample preserves
   the INF (\`INF + finite = INF\`).
4. Line 348-349 \`if value > self.max { self.max = value }\`. Initial
   \`max = NEG_INFINITY\` so \`INF > NEG_INFINITY\` is true → \`max = INF\`.
   Every later finite value compares \`finite > INF\` → false, so \`max\`
   stays at INF forever.
5. \`Histogram::mean()\` (line 401) returns \`INF / count = INF\`.
6. \`Histogram::summary()\` returns \`Some(INF)\` for \`mean\`, \`max\`, and
   potentially \`p99\`. \`HistogramSummary\` is \`Serialize\`; serde_json's
   default serializer emits \`null\` for non-finite f64 (RFC 8259 forbids
   Inf in JSON), silently dropping the value from telemetry payloads
   that downstream dashboards consume.
7. Symmetric for \`record(f64::NEG_INFINITY)\` — \`total_sum\` and \`min\`
   become NEG_INFINITY permanently, mean reports \`-INF\`.

## Severity rationale

- Same poisoning shape as ft-b4l62 (NaN), ft-761tz (NaN in
  disk_pressure), ft-yskcu (NaN in continuous_backpressure), ft-7s0vw
  (overflow in event_id) — silent wrong answer with no self-recovery.
- Existing NaN guard makes the absence of an Inf guard surprising:
  reviewers reading the code see \`is_nan()\` and assume the histogram
  is hardened, when it is not.
- Reachable via legitimate division-by-zero in any client computing
  durations or rates as f64.

## Fix sketch

1. \`Histogram::record\` (line 340) — broaden the guard from
   \`if value.is_nan()\` to \`if !value.is_finite()\`. \`f64::is_finite\`
   is true iff the value is neither NaN nor ±Infinity.
2. Update the doc comment and \`[ft-b4l62]\` reference to note that the
   guard now covers Inf as well.
3. Regression tests covering: (a) record(INFINITY) is no-op,
   total_sum stays 0.0, mean stays None; (b) record(NEG_INFINITY) is
   no-op; (c) record(finite) then record(INFINITY) then record(finite)
   — second finite blends correctly without Inf contamination;
   (d) summary().max stays Some(finite_value) after an Inf attempt.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/telemetry.rs\` only. No cross-crate API
changes. \`HistogramSummary\` shape unchanged.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:58:02.402142Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:06:14.413298Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:06:14.413032Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit f73e139a: Histogram::record now uses !value.is_finite() to reject both NaN and ±INFINITY. 4 regression tests added covering pos-Inf, neg-Inf, all-Inf, and summary().max stays finite after Inf attempt. All 74 histogram-related tests pass against /tmp/ft-rv-p0-target. Pattern matches the arithmetic-poison family alongside ft-b4l62 (NaN guard, original), ft-761tz, ft-yskcu, ft-7s0vw.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
